result:
The silence of the room is a physical thing, a held breath. Outside, a single car hisses by on wet asphalt, a sound that only deepens the quiet it briefly slices through. It’s in this suspension that the mind begins to wander, not along the neat footpaths of intention, but through the overgrown woods of *whatever comes*.

What comes first is the memory of my grandmother’s hands. Not a sentimental, soft-focus image, but the specific topography of them: the ropy blue veins rising over knuckles swollen like burls on an old oak, the brown sunspots like maps of forgotten continents, the nails always trimmed short and practical. Those hands could knead dough into silk, could thread a needle in lamplight, could point with terrifying authority. They were a record of 80 years of work—scrubbing, planting, soothing, gripping. I think of my own hands, tapping on this glass screen, and feel a pang of something like guilt for their smooth, unmarked idleness. What story will they tell? A story of tapping, scrolling, clicking. It seems insufficient, somehow.

This thought spirals out to the idea of legacies, the things we leave in our wake. Not the grand monuments, but the minor archaeologies: the worn spot on a wooden stair, the specific way we fold a towel, a favourite phrase adopted by a friend. We are all slowly terraforming the tiny planets of our lives, and the people who share our orbit. My old physics teacher, Mr. Hendricks, had a habit of saying “Right, then!” when a concept finally clicked for a student. I catch myself saying it now, decades later, to an empty room, and he is there again, chalk dust on his elbow, making the universe feel comprehensible. He left that in me.

The rain starts again, a gentle percussion on the roof. It makes me think of water cycles—how the same molecules that once fell on dinosaurs, that coursed through Roman aqueducts, that a Viking drank from a northern stream, are now drumming on my roof, will soon trickle through the gutters, seep into the earth, be drawn up by the roots of the maple tree, transpired into the sky to begin again. We are all just temporary containers for ancient water and stardust. The carbon in my cells was forged in the heart of a dying star. The iron in my blood was once part of a meteorite. We are not just *in* the universe; we are *of* it, literal pieces of it that have, for a fleeting moment, become self-aware.

This awareness feels both magnificent and unbearably lonely. Here I am, a collection of recycled cosmic material, sitting in a room, thinking about thinking. What is the *I* that observes these thoughts? Is it the neural network, the electricity flickering along a biological substrate? Or is it something else, the silent space in which the thoughts appear, like clouds in a vast sky? The *whatever comes* comes from somewhere, but where does the watcher of it reside?

A sudden, sharp smell of woodsmoke from a neighbour’s chimney cuts through the abstraction. It is immediate, primal. It pulls me from the cosmos back to this street, this evening, this animal body. The body that is hungry, that feels the cool air from the window, that is soothed by the smell of burning oak. We spend so much time in the empires of our minds, building and toppling worries, replaying conversations, projecting futures, that we forget the simple kingdom of the senses. The bitter tang of coffee. The crushing softness of a deep pile carpet under bare feet. The almost painful beauty of a certain chord in a song, a beauty that feels like homesickness for a place you’ve never been.

And that’s the thread: homesickness. Not for a house, but for a state of being. A feeling I can only describe as *fitting*. The seamless moment when you are not separate from what you are doing—laughing so hard you can’t breathe, lost in a task, holding a sleeping child, watching a bird on a branch. The ego, that noisy narrator, shuts off, and there is just… being. Perhaps this is what all the seeking is about: prayer, art, love, even addiction. A desperate attempt to shut off the narrator and return, just for a second, to that felt sense of belonging.

It’s getting dark now. The room’s features are softening into shadows. The *whatever comes* begins to turn towards the practical, the mundane—a reminder to buy milk, a mental note for a work email tomorrow. The grand reverie is being gently herded back into its pen by the demands of the everyday. And maybe that’s the final truth that comes. This dance between the transcendent and the trivial is the human condition. We are the creatures who can contemplate infinity one moment and be utterly derailed by a misplaced set of keys the next.

We live in the tension between the hands that planted potatoes and the hands that manipulate virtual realities. Between the stardust and the shopping list. Between the profound silence at the core of our being and the relentless, beautiful noise of being alive.

The last car passes. The rain has stopped. The world outside is a palette of deep blues and bruised purples, dotted with the jewel-toned lights of windows where other containers of stardust and ancient water are having their own dinners, their own thoughts, their own silent reckonings. My 1000 words are nearly up. The allotted space for this wandering is closing.

But the mind, of course, doesn’t stop. It just hums along, a generator in the basement of the self, producing whatever comes next. The only real task, perhaps, is to listen to it now and then—not to control its output, but to witness its strange and meandering production with curiosity, and maybe a little gratitude. For in the end, this chaotic, associative, deeply trivial and occasionally luminous stream is the proof that the generator is still on, the lights are still warm, and the vessel, for now, is still wonderfully, messily, alive.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
